sections.content: Overview

Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) is a foundational paradigm that structures code around objects—self-contained bundles of data and behaviour—rather than loose functions and variables. In machine learning and AI systems, codebases grow exponentially in complexity. Without OOP principles, managing thousands of interrelated functions becomes chaotic: duplicate logic spreads across files, changes break multiple systems simultaneously, and testing becomes nearly impossible. OOP solves this through encapsulation (bundling data with methods that operate on it), inheritance (reusing and specializing behaviour across related objects), and polymorphism (allowing different objects to respond to the same operation differently). In production ML frameworks like TensorFlow and PyTorch, every neural network layer is an object with its own forward pass logic, parameters, and gradient computation. Without OOP, these libraries would be unmaintainable. The alternative—procedural or functional styles at large scale—leads to brittle, tightly-coupled code where a single bug in shared state cascades through the entire system. Modern AI pipelines depend on OOP to maintain clarity, testability, and scalability across millions of lines of code.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: In a Test match, Virat Kohli as captain must manage 11 players on the field—each with specific skills, responsibilities, and records. He doesn't micromanage every batting stroke or bowling delivery; instead, he groups players by role (batsmen, bowlers, fielders). Each batsman maintains their own statistics (career runs, average, centuries), each bowler tracks their own metrics (wickets, economy rate, strike rate). When Kohli asks Jasprit Bumrah to bowl a spell, Bumrah doesn't need to be reinvented every over—he already knows how to run up, grip the ball, and execute deliveries based on his training. When Rohit Sharma opens the batting, his technique and experience carry over from previous innings automatically. Objects in OOP work identically: a CricketPlayer object encapsulates all the data (name, runs, wickets, average) and methods (bat(), bowl(), field()) for that player. Inheritance means specialized player types (FastBowler, OpeningBatsman) inherit from Player but customize their behaviour. Polymorphism lets you call player.perform() and it automatically does the right action—Bumrah bowls, Kohli bats—without you needing to know their type. Understanding OOP reveals why frameworks can manage hundreds of neural network layers as objects: each layer is independent, reusable, and knows how to do its job without the entire system needing to change.
Core Concepts
Classes and Objects

A class is a blueprint or template for creating objects. It defines the structure (attributes) and behaviour (methods) that every instance of that class will have. In Python, a class is defined with the `class` keyword and specifies what data each object should store and what operations it can perform. An object is a concrete instantiation of that class—a specific, individual entity with particular values for its attributes. For example, a `CricketPlayer` class defines that every player should have a name, jersey number, runs scored, and wickets taken, plus methods to update these values. When you create an object `player1 = CricketPlayer('Rohit Sharma', 45)`, you now have a specific player with those exact attributes. Multiple objects from the same class are independent: changing one player's runs doesn't affect another's. In machine learning, this distinction is critical. A `NeuralNetworkLayer` class defines the general structure of a layer (weights, biases, activation function), but each instantiated layer is a separate object with its own parameter matrices. Without this separation, you couldn't build multi-layer networks—every layer would interfere with every other. Classes enable reusability, organization, and the ability to work with many similar but distinct entities in a single program.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: A cricket team's roster is like a class. It's the blueprint: "A batsman must have a name, jersey number, runs scored in this innings, and an average." When the team sheet is announced, Rohit Sharma is one object instantiated from that blueprint—he's a specific player with 45 as his jersey, 4000 career runs, and a 47.5 average. His teammate Virat Kohli is another object from the same class with different attribute values (18 jersey, 8000 runs, 48.3 average). Both follow the same class structure, but each maintains independent state. When Rohit scores 89 runs in an innings, it only updates his object's runs attribute—it doesn't change Kohli's record. A class is like the team manual; objects are the actual players playing on a given day. In machine learning, when you create multiple LSTM layers for a sequence model, you instantiate the LSTM class multiple times. Each layer object has its own weight matrices and bias vectors, completely independent. One layer's learning doesn't affect another's. This instantiation-from-template pattern is why you can dynamically build neural networks with loops—each iteration creates a fresh, independent object from the same class blueprint.
Encapsulation

Encapsulation is the principle of bundling data (attributes) and methods that operate on that data together inside a class, and hiding internal implementation details from the outside world. In Python, this is enforced through access modifiers: public attributes/methods (no underscore prefix) are accessible everywhere, protected attributes (single underscore prefix like `_runs`) are conventionally for internal use, and private attributes (double underscore prefix like `__runs`) are name-mangled to prevent direct external access. Encapsulation serves multiple critical purposes. First, it prevents external code from corrupting an object's state. A CricketPlayer's runs attribute shouldn't be directly modified from outside the class; instead, it should be updated only through controlled methods like `record_runs()` which can validate inputs. Second, it allows changing internal representation without affecting code that uses the class. If you decide to store `_runs_history` as a list internally instead of a single total, all external code using the public `get_runs()` method continues working unchanged. Third, encapsulation clarifies the contract: users of your class know exactly what methods are stable, public interfaces and which internals they shouldn't rely on. In machine learning systems, this is crucial. A neural network layer's forward pass must be protected from direct manipulation; only the public `forward()` method should be called, ensuring that input validation, shape checking, and device placement happen correctly. Without encapsulation, external code could corrupt the layer's internal state, causing silent bugs in model training.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: A cricket match's scorecard is encapsulated. The public interface is what fans see on the screen: runs, wickets, overs, extras. Behind that, the match officials and commentators have access to protected details: the exact delivery data, pitch conditions, decision review outcomes. The data entry operators have private access to raw sensor readings and communication channels—fans can't directly manipulate these because doing so would corrupt the entire scorecard. When MS Dhoni's team scores 45 runs in an over, the scorekeeper uses the official method to record it. They don't directly access and modify the runs counter—they follow procedure, validate that the over is valid, update the scorecard, and broadcast the public result. If later the data format changes from storing each run separately to storing cumulative runs, the public interface (what's displayed) remains the same; the scorekeeper's process doesn't change. In TensorFlow, a Dense layer encapsulates its weights and biases privately. You don't access `layer.__weights` directly; you call `layer(input_tensor)` which handles everything internally. If TensorFlow engineers later optimize weight storage using sparse matrices internally, your code calling `layer()` breaks nothing. This is encapsulation's power: stability and flexibility through controlled access.
Inheritance

Inheritance is a mechanism that allows a new class (child or derived class) to inherit attributes and methods from an existing class (parent or base class), promoting code reuse and establishing hierarchical relationships. When you create a subclass using `class SpecializedClass(ParentClass):`, the child automatically gains all public and protected members of the parent. The child can then override parent methods to specialize behaviour or add entirely new methods. Inheritance is powerful because it eliminates duplication. In cricket, all players have common attributes (name, jersey number, career statistics) and methods (get_stats(), update_runs()). Rather than copying this code into separate `Batsman` and `Bowler` classes, you define it once in a parent `CricketPlayer` class. Then `Bowler` extends `CricketPlayer` and adds bowling-specific attributes (economy_rate, maiden_overs) and methods (bowl(), update_wickets()). In machine learning, inheritance is fundamental to framework architecture. PyTorch defines a base `nn.Module` class with common functionality for all layers. Every specific layer type—Linear, Conv2d, LSTM, Transformer—inherits from Module and only implements its unique forward logic. If PyTorch engineers improve how Module manages device placement or gradient computation, all 50+ layer types automatically benefit. Without inheritance, each layer would need to reimplement these features separately, creating maintenance nightmares and inconsistent behaviour. Inheritance also enables polymorphism: code that expects a `nn.Module` can work with any specific layer type without knowing which one. This is why neural network models can work with pluggable components.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: A cricket team has a hierarchy. All players inherit from a base Player class—they all have name, jersey number, playing history, and a method to get their career stats. But Jasprit Bumrah as a FastBowler inherits from Player and adds FastBowler-specific attributes: bowling speed (145+ km/h), yorker accuracy, and the yorker() method. Rohit Sharma as an OpeningBatsman inherits from Player and adds OpeningBatsman-specific attributes: powerplay strike rate and opening_partnership() method. When the coach calls a method like player.perform(), each type responds appropriately—Bumrah executes a bowling spell at 150 km/h, Rohit plays aggressive strokes in the powerplay. This inheritance structure means if team management decides all players must now track their physical fitness score, they update the base Player class once and all players (Bowlers, Batsmen, Fielders) immediately have that feature. In PyTorch, a Conv2d layer inherits from nn.Module. Module provides the framework for parameter registration, training/eval modes, and moving to GPU. Conv2d only implements the specific forward pass math (convolution). If someone writes code that accepts any nn.Module and calls module.to(device), it works perfectly on Conv2d, Linear, LSTM, or any future layer invented. This is the power of inheritance—specialization without duplication, and polymorphic code that works across the entire hierarchy.
Polymorphism

Polymorphism is the ability of objects of different types to respond to the same method call in their own specific way. The word literally means "many forms." In Python, polymorphism is achieved through method overriding: a child class redefines a method inherited from a parent class with its own implementation. When you call the method on an object, Python automatically determines which version to execute based on the object's actual type at runtime, not its declared type. This is called dynamic dispatch or late binding. For example, both `Batsman` and `Bowler` might inherit from `Player` and override a `perform()` method: Batsman's perform() executes a batting sequence, while Bowler's perform() executes a bowling spell. If you have a list of mixed Player types and call `player.perform()` on each, the correct specialization runs automatically. Polymorphism enables writing generic, flexible code. In machine learning, every neural network layer implements a `forward()` method. When building a model, you don't need different code for different layer types—you iterate through layers and call `layer.forward(x)`. Whether the layer is Linear, Conv2d, or BatchNorm, the correct computation happens transparently. This is why TensorFlow models can be defined in `Sequential()` as a stack of generic layers, and the framework handles routing data to the right layer implementation. Without polymorphism, you'd need explicit if-elif chains checking layer types, which is fragile and unscalable. Polymorphism is what allows frameworks to evolve: developers can create new layer types that inherit from the base Module class and override forward(), and existing model code works unchanged.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: The captain's playbook includes a strategy call: "Execute your role." When the captain says this during an innings, Virat Kohli (batsman) executes his role—he faces the bowler and tries to score runs. When the same captain says "Execute your role" to Jasprit Bumrah (bowler), Bumrah executes his completely different role—he runs up and bowls deliveries aiming for wickets. Same method name, same signal from the captain, but different actual execution based on who receives it. The captain doesn't need to know the details of how Kohli bats or how Bumrah bowls; he just triggers the action and each player knows what to do. In a T20 powerplay, the captain might call out "PlayAggressively()" to the entire batting lineup. Each batsman responds differently: Rohit might hit boundaries aggressively, while a tail-ender might focus on singles. Same method, different implementations based on player type. In neural networks, when you have a model with mixed layer types and call `model.forward(batch)`, internally it iterates through layers and calls `layer.forward(batch)` on each. A Conv2d layer performs 2D convolution, a Linear layer performs matrix multiplication, an LSTM layer performs sequential gating. Same message, completely different operations. This is polymorphism's elegance: generic interfaces that route to specialized implementations, allowing frameworks to grow without rewriting caller code.

# Object-Oriented Programming Fundamentals in Cricket

from datetime import datetime
from typing import List

# Base class demonstrating encapsulation and inheritance foundation
class CricketPlayer:
    """Base class for all cricket players."""
    
    def __init__(self, name: str, jersey_num: int, country: str):
        # Public attributes
        self.name = name
        self.jersey_num = jersey_num
        self.country = country
        
        # Protected attributes (convention: internal use)
        self._career_matches = 0
        self._career_runs = 0
        self._career_wickets = 0
        
        # Private attributes (name-mangled)
        self.__date_of_birth = None
    
    def get_stats(self) -> dict:
        """Public method to retrieve player statistics."""
        return {
            "name": self.name,
            "jersey": self.jersey_num,
            "matches": self._career_matches,
            "runs": self._career_runs,
            "wickets": self._career_wickets
        }
    
    def _update_matches(self):
        """Protected method: increment match count."""
        self._career_matches += 1
    
    def perform(self) -> str:
        """Polymorphic method - overridden by subclasses."""
        return f"{self.name} prepares to play."


# Inheritance and polymorphism: Batsman specializes Player
class Batsman(CricketPlayer):
    """Batsman class inheriting from CricketPlayer."""
    
    def __init__(self, name: str, jersey_num: int, country: str, batting_style: str):
        super().__init__(name, jersey_num, country)
        self.batting_style = batting_style  # Aggressive, Defensive, All-rounder
        self._highest_score = 0
        self._centuries = 0
    
    def record_runs(self, runs: int) -> None:
        """Encapsulated method to record runs (validates input)."""
        if runs < 0:
            raise ValueError("Runs cannot be negative")
        self._career_runs += runs
        if runs >= 100:
            self._centuries += 1
    
    def perform(self) -> str:
        """Polymorphic override: batsman's specific action."""
        self._update_matches()
        return f"{self.name} ({self.batting_style}) takes guard and faces the bowler. Career runs: {self._career_runs}"
    
    def get_stats(self) -> dict:
        """Override parent method to include batting-specific stats."""
        stats = super().get_stats()
        stats.update({
            "batting_style": self.batting_style,
            "centuries": self._centuries,
            "highest_score": self._highest_score
        })
        return stats


# Inheritance and polymorphism: Bowler specializes Player
class Bowler(CricketPlayer):
    """Bowler class inheriting from CricketPlayer."""
    
    def __init__(self, name: str, jersey_num: int, country: str, bowling_type: str):
        super().__init__(name, jersey_num, country)
        self.bowling_type = bowling_type  # FastBowl, Spinner, AllRounder
        self._maiden_overs = 0
        self._economy_rate = 0.0
    
    def record_wicket(self) -> None:
        """Encapsulated method to record a wicket."""
        self._career_wickets += 1
    
    def record_maiden_over(self) -> None:
        """Record a maiden over (no runs conceded)."""
        self._maiden_overs += 1
    
    def perform(self) -> str:
        """Polymorphic override: bowler's specific action."""
        self._update_matches()
        return f"{self.name} ({self.bowling_type}) runs up and bowls. Career wickets: {self._career_wickets}"
    
    def get_stats(self) -> dict:
        """Override parent method to include bowling-specific stats."""
        stats = super().get_stats()
        stats.update({
            "bowling_type": self.bowling_type,
            "maiden_overs": self._maiden_overs,
            "economy_rate": self._economy_rate
        })
        return stats


# Team management class demonstrating composition and polymorphism
class CricketTeam:
    """A cricket team manages multiple players of different types."""
    
    def __init__(self, team_name: str, country: str):
        self.team_name = team_name
        self.country = country
        self._players: List[CricketPlayer] = []
        self._match_date = None
    
    def add_player(self, player: CricketPlayer) -> None:
        """Add a player to the team."""
        self._players.append(player)
    
    def execute_match_day(self) -> List[str]:
        """Polymorphic iteration: each player performs according to their type."""
        actions = []
        actions.append(f"\n=== {self.team_name} ({self.country}) - Match Day ===")
        
        # Polymorphism in action: same method call, different behaviors
        for player in self._players:
            action = player.perform()  # Dynamic dispatch based on actual type
            actions.append(action)
        
        return actions
    
    def get_team_stats(self) -> dict:
        """Aggregate statistics for all players."""
        total_runs = sum(p._career_runs for p in self._players)
        total_wickets = sum(p._career_wickets for p in self._players)
        return {
            "team": self.team_name,
            "players_count": len(self._players),
            "total_runs": total_runs,
            "total_wickets": total_wickets
        }


# Demonstration
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Create individual players (objects instantiated from classes)
    rohit = Batsman("Rohit Sharma", 45, "India", "Aggressive")
    kohli = Batsman("Virat Kohli", 18, "India", "All-rounder")
    bumrah = Bowler("Jasprit Bumrah", 93, "India", "FastBowl")
    siraj = Bowler("Mohammad Siraj", 25, "India", "FastBowl")
    
    # Record some career statistics (encapsulated updates)
    rohit.record_runs(89)
    rohit.record_runs(120)
    kohli.record_runs(95)
    kohli.record_runs(45)
    
    bumrah.record_wicket()
    bumrah.record_wicket()
    bumrah.record_maiden_over()
    siraj.record_wicket()
    
    # Create a team and add players
    india_team = CricketTeam("India", "India")
    india_team.add_player(rohit)
    india_team.add_player(kohli)
    india_team.add_player(bumrah)
    india_team.add_player(siraj)
    
    # Execute match day (polymorphism: each player type responds differently)
    match_actions = india_team.execute_match_day()
    for action in match_actions:
        print(action)
    
    # Display individual player stats (encapsulated access)
    print("\n=== Player Statistics ===")
    print(rohit.get_stats())
    print(bumrah.get_stats())
    
    # Display team aggregates
    print("\n=== Team Aggregates ===")
    print(india_team.get_team_stats())

The code demonstrates OOP fundamentals in a cricket context. We start with a base `CricketPlayer` class (encapsulation and foundation for inheritance) that encapsulates player data (name, jersey, career stats) with controlled methods like `get_stats()`. The class uses access modifiers: public attributes like name, protected `_career_runs` for internal tracking, and a private `__date_of_birth` to show name-mangling. Two derived classes—`Batsman` and `Bowler`—inherit from `CricketPlayer` and specialize behaviour. Batsman adds `record_runs()` method and tracks centuries; Bowler adds `record_wicket()` and maiden overs. Both override the `perform()` method, demonstrating polymorphism: when `player.perform()` is called on a mixed list of players in `execute_match_day()`, Python automatically calls the correct implementation—Batsman.perform() for batsmen, Bowler.perform() for bowlers. The `CricketTeam` class demonstrates composition: it manages multiple CricketPlayer objects and iterates through them polymorphically. When we create rohit as a Batsman and bumrah as a Bowler, they're independent objects with separate state. Calling rohit.record_runs(89) modifies only Rohit's runs, not Bumrah's. The team statistics aggregation shows how polymorphism eliminates type-checking: we simply iterate and call methods, trusting Python's dynamic dispatch to route to the right implementation. This mirrors how machine learning frameworks work: a model contains layers of mixed types, and iterating through `forward()` calls works seamlessly.
How It Works Under the Hood

When Python executes an OOP program, several mechanisms work together at runtime. First, when you define a class, Python creates a class object in memory—a template containing method definitions and shared attributes. When you instantiate an object like `player = Batsman('Rohit', 45, 'India', 'Aggressive')`, Python allocates memory for the instance's unique attributes and creates a reference to its class. The object maintains an internal `__dict__` (a dictionary) storing instance attributes. Method calls use dynamic dispatch: when you call `player.perform()`, Python first checks if `perform` exists in the Batsman class. If not found, it walks up the Method Resolution Order (MRO)—the inheritance chain—checking parent classes until it finds the method. The MRO in Python follows C3 linearization, a specific algorithm ensuring consistent resolution in multiple inheritance. For encapsulation, name-mangling occurs at class definition time: `__private_attr` is automatically renamed to `_ClassName__private_attr`. When you try to access `player.__date_of_birth` from outside, it fails because Python looks for that exact name, and it doesn't exist; only `_CricketPlayer__date_of_birth` exists. This is not true access restriction but rather a convention with light enforcement. Protected attributes with single underscores rely entirely on convention; Python doesn't prevent access. Polymorphism works through late binding: at call time, Python doesn't pre-determine which method to execute. Instead, it looks up the method on the actual object's class at runtime. If a Batsman and Bowler both have a `perform()` method, calling `player.perform()` on either executes the correct version because Python resolved the actual type dynamically. Memory efficiency comes from class-level method sharing: all Batsman instances share the same `record_runs` method code; only instance attributes differ. Inheritance uses a method resolution order to find overridden methods efficiently. Complex inheritance hierarchies can have deeper MRO chains, incurring small lookup overhead, but Python's C-level caching minimizes this. In machine learning frameworks, this runtime polymorphism enables layers to be defined generically but executed type-specifically, all without runtime overhead from explicit type checks.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: A cricket match's execution mirrors Python's OOP runtime. The match structure (defined rules, field layout, player roles) is like a class—it exists as the blueprint before any specific match starts. When a match is played on, say, January 15th at the MCG with Rohit Sharma and Bumrah on the field, that's an instantiation—a specific object with unique state (current score, wickets, overs). The umpire's decision process resembles Python's MRO: when a close LBW decision must be made, the umpire checks the obvious rule (does the ball hit the pads in line with stumps?). If unclear, they check the next rule layer (is the batsman attempting to play the ball?), then the next (is there doubt?), creating a chain of checks. If any rule answers the question, that's it—no need to check deeper. Similarly, Python walks up the inheritance chain checking for methods. When the captain gives a signal like "Execute powerplay strategy," each player responds according to their role—batsmen play aggressively, bowlers bowl attacking spells, fielders set attacking fields. This is polymorphism: same instruction, different implementations based on player type, all happening simultaneously without the captain knowing specifics. The captain doesn't need to pre-decide "Kohli will hit sixes, Sharma will take singles"—he just gives the command and the right actions happen. Method resolution order is like the ICC cricket rulebook hierarchy: specific format rules (T20 has powerplay overs 1-6), then general Rules of Cricket, then interpretations. When a match situation arises, the umpire follows this order until finding an applicable rule. This ordered search parallels how Python finds methods up the inheritance chain.

# Advanced OOP: Method Resolution Order (MRO), Multiple Inheritance, and Metaclass concepts

from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
from typing import List, Optional

# Demonstrating MRO and abstract base classes
class Player(ABC):
    """Abstract base class - cannot be instantiated directly."""
    
    def __init__(self, name: str, jersey: int):
        self.name = name
        self.jersey = jersey
        self._matches_played = 0
    
    @abstractmethod
    def execute_role(self) -> str:
        """Abstract method - must be overridden by subclasses."""
        pass
    
    @abstractmethod
    def get_primary_stat(self) -> int:
        """Abstract method - must implement primary performance stat."""
        pass
    
    def log_match(self) -> None:
        """Concrete method available to all subclasses."""
        self._matches_played += 1
        print(f"{self.name} played match #{self._matches_played}")


# Mixin class for additional functionality (composition pattern)
class CaptainMixin:
    """Mixin to grant captain responsibilities."""
    
    def __init__(self):
        self._team_decisions = []
    
    def make_decision(self, decision: str) -> None:
        """Captain makes a strategic decision."""
        self._team_decisions.append(decision)
        print(f"Captain Decision: {decision}")
    
    def get_decisions(self) -> List[str]:
        """Retrieve decision history."""
        return self._team_decisions


class CourtlyBatsman(Player, CaptainMixin):
    """Batsman who is also captain - demonstrates multiple inheritance."""
    
    def __init__(self, name: str, jersey: int, batting_avg: float):
        Player.__init__(self, name, jersey)
        CaptainMixin.__init__(self)  # Initialize mixin
        self._batting_average = batting_avg
        self._runs_in_match = 0
    
    def execute_role(self) -> str:
        """Polymorphic implementation for batsman-captain."""
        self.log_match()
        return f"{self.name} (Captain) bats with average {self._batting_average:.1f}"
    
    def get_primary_stat(self) -> int:
        """Batsman's primary stat is runs."""
        return int(self._batting_average * 50)  # Estimated runs
    
    def score_runs(self, runs: int) -> None:
        """Encapsulated method to record runs."""
        if runs >= 0:
            self._runs_in_match += runs


class PacerBowler(Player):
    """Fast bowler specialization."""
    
    def __init__(self, name: str, jersey: int, speed_kmh: float):
        super().__init__(name, jersey)
        self._speed_kmh = speed_kmh
        self._wickets_taken = 0
    
    def execute_role(self) -> str:
        """Polymorphic implementation for bowler."""
        self.log_match()
        return f"{self.name} bowls fast at {self._speed_kmh:.1f} km/h"
    
    def get_primary_stat(self) -> int:
        """Bowler's primary stat is wickets."""
        return self._wickets_taken
    
    def take_wicket(self) -> None:
        """Record a wicket."""
        self._wickets_taken += 1
        print(f"{self.name} takes a wicket! Total: {self._wickets_taken}")


class Match:
    """Match coordinator - manages MRO in action."""
    
    def __init__(self, match_name: str):
        self.match_name = match_name
        self._players: List[Player] = []
    
    def register_player(self, player: Player) -> None:
        """Register a player in the match."""
        self._players.append(player)
    
    def simulate_match_actions(self) -> None:
        """Execute polymorphic method calls - MRO in action."""
        print(f"\n=== {self.match_name} ===")
        print(f"MRO Example: Testing polymorphism across {len(self._players)} players\n")
        
        for player in self._players:
            # Late binding: actual method depends on runtime type
            print(player.execute_role())
            primary_stat = player.get_primary_stat()
            print(f"  Primary Stat Value: {primary_stat}")
            
            # If captain, show decisions
            if isinstance(player, CaptainMixin):
                decisions = player.get_decisions()
                if decisions:
                    print(f"  Decisions Made: {decisions}")
            print()
    
    def print_mro_for_player(self, player: Player) -> None:
        """Demonstrate Method Resolution Order."""
        print(f"\nMethod Resolution Order (MRO) for {player.__class__.__name__}:")
        for i, cls in enumerate(player.__class__.__mro__):
            print(f"  {i}. {cls.__name__}")


# Demonstration of advanced concepts
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Create instances
    virat = CourtlyBatsman("Virat Kohli", 18, 48.3)
    jasprit = PacerBowler("Jasprit Bumrah", 93, 148.5)
    rohit = CourtlyBatsman("Rohit Sharma", 45, 46.8)
    
    # Captain makes strategic decisions (mixin in action)
    virat.make_decision("Accelerate in powerplay")
    virat.make_decision("Bowl short deliveries at death")
    
    # Simulate match
    match = Match("India vs Australia - Test Match")
    match.register_player(virat)
    match.register_player(jasprit)
    match.register_player(rohit)
    
    match.simulate_match_actions()
    
    # Show MRO for different classes
    match.print_mro_for_player(virat)
    match.print_mro_for_player(jasprit)
    
    # Score some runs and wickets
    print("\n=== In-Match Actions ===")
    virat.score_runs(45)
    print(f"Virat scores 45 runs. Match total: {virat._runs_in_match}")
    
    jasprit.take_wicket()
    jasprit.take_wicket()
    
    # Demonstrate polymorphism with abstract class
    print("\n=== Polymorphic Iteration ===")
    all_players: List[Player] = [virat, jasprit, rohit]
    total_stats = sum(p.get_primary_stat() for p in all_players)
    print(f"Combined primary stats across all players: {total_stats}")
    print("\nNote: get_primary_stat() returns runs for batsmen, wickets for bowlers")
    print("This is polymorphism: same method call, different meanings per type.")

Pro Tip: In production machine learning systems, always design your base classes around the most common interface (like nn.Module in PyTorch for all layers). This allows the framework to handle thousands of custom layer implementations without modification. Use abstract base classes (ABC and @abstractmethod) to enforce that subclasses implement critical methods like forward(). This prevents silent bugs where a developer forgets to override a required method. Additionally, prefer composition over deep inheritance hierarchies. A neural network with 5+ levels of inheritance becomes hard to reason about. Instead, use mixins sparingly and composition (having objects contain other objects) for flexibility. This is why modern frameworks use relatively flat inheritance (Layer inherits from Module, specific layers inherit from Layer) rather than complex trees.
Common Patterns & Best Practices

Several established patterns emerge from years of OOP practice in production systems. First, the Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) states that a class should have one reason to change. A CricketPlayer class should manage player data and behaviour; it shouldn't also manage team finances or stadium scheduling. When SRP is violated, changes to one concern (e.g., salary structure) force modifications to unrelated code. In machine learning, a DataLoader class should handle loading and batching data; it shouldn't also validate model architecture. A Layer class should implement forward computation; it shouldn't manage training loops or checkpointing. Second, the Dependency Inversion Principle suggests depending on abstractions, not concrete implementations. Instead of a Model class directly creating specific layer instances, pass them as constructor arguments. This allows testing with mock layers and swapping implementations without changing Model code. Third, composition over inheritance: prefer giving a class a reference to another object rather than inheriting from it. A CricketTeam should contain CricketPlayer objects, not inherit from Player. This is more flexible because a team can contain mixed player types without creating awkward inheritance hierarchies. In TensorFlow, a Sequential model contains Layer objects; layers don't inherit from Sequential. Finally, the Template Method pattern is useful for enforcing consistent workflows. A base Optimizer class defines the optimization loop (get_gradients, apply_updates, update_params) but leaves specific gradient computation abstract. All optimizer subclasses (SGD, Adam, RMSprop) inherit the loop and implement only their unique gradient/update logic. This ensures consistency while allowing specialization.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: The team performance depends on strong fundamentals, not complex organizational structures. Single Responsibility Principle: The opening batsman's job is to bat aggressively in the first 6 overs; he shouldn't also manage team logistics or make bowling changes. If he's responsible for too much, errors spread everywhere. Similarly, a data validation class shouldn't also perform model training—one responsibility each. Dependency Inversion: The captain doesn't hard-code "Rohit must open"; instead, the opening slot is a flexible role. When Rohit is injured, any qualified opening batsman fits in without redesigning the entire team structure. The captain depends on the abstract role, not a concrete player. Composition: A team is built by assembling individual players, not inheriting from them. A cricket team contains 11 players; it doesn't inherit player behaviour. If you tried to make CricketTeam inherit from Player, it would be nonsensical. Template Method: The match structure (innings, overs, wickets) is like a template. The template dictates "batsmen bat, bowlers bowl, you follow the rules," but specific strategies are pluggable. Each batsman uses the same match template but executes their unique style within it. Virat and Rohit both follow the match structure (same template) but bat differently (different implementations). Understanding these patterns reveals why successful frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow, Keras) have remained stable across versions: they follow SRP (layers do one thing), composition (models contain layers), and template methods (all layers follow the same forward interface), making them extensible without breaking existing code.

Warning: The most common OOP mistake beginners make is deep inheritance hierarchies to model every slight variation. For example, creating Player → Cricketer → Batsman → OpeningBatsman → AggressiveOpeningBatsman is wrong. This creates fragile code where changes to a parent class break all descendants, and each new variation requires a new class. The fix: use composition and mixins instead. Have a Player class with a stats_tracker attribute (composition), role attribute ("opener", "middle-order"), and style attribute ("aggressive", "defensive"). This is far more flexible. If requirements change and you need a "middle-order aggressive batsman who also keeps wickets," it's trivial with composition (just set attributes) but painful with deep inheritance (create a new class inheriting from multiple parents). Additionally, never inherit just to reuse code. If you're copying a parent's method unchanged in the child, you're likely violating DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) and should refactor into a standalone function or composition. Inheritance should model is-a relationships (Bowler is-a Player), not code sharing.
Real-World Application

Object-Oriented Programming is the backbone of every major machine learning and AI framework. TensorFlow's entire architecture is built on OOP: every operation, layer, and model is an object inheriting from base classes. The `tf.keras.layers.Layer` base class defines the interface (build, call, get_config) that hundreds of specialized layer types override. Without OOP, implementing Conv2D, LSTM, Transformer, and BatchNorm would require duplicate code for weight initialization, gradient computation, serialization, and device placement. PyTorch similarly uses `nn.Module` as the base: every layer inherits from Module and overrides `forward()`. This design allows researchers to implement novel layers in 10 lines of code by inheriting from Module, yet they automatically gain distributed training support, mixed precision computation, and checkpoint compatibility—all handled by the base class. scikit-learn follows the same pattern: all estimators (classifiers, regressors, transformers) inherit from `BaseEstimator` and `RegressorMixin` or `ClassifierMixin`. This enforces a consistent interface (fit, predict, transform) so any algorithm can plug into pipelines without modification. In production systems at scale, OOP enables rapid experimentation. At companies like Google, Meta, and OpenAI, teams can define new model architectures by combining existing layer objects without touching framework code. A researcher working on multi-head attention only needs to write the attention logic; encapsulation and inheritance handle everything else. Without OOP, adding each new layer type would require modifying hundreds of framework files, introducing bugs and slowing development. Large-scale systems use composition and polymorphism to manage complexity: a trainer object composes dataset, model, optimizer, and loss objects. Each can be swapped independently. Training code remains one generic loop calling polymorphic methods (dataset.get_batch(), model.forward(), loss.compute()), but the actual implementation comes from specific subclass instances chosen at runtime.

items: Classes define templates for objects; objects are instances with specific attribute values. Instantiating multiple objects from one class allows managing thousands of similar but independent entities—fundamental for managing datasets, model layers, and training state in production ML systems.

Encapsulation hides internal implementation behind public method interfaces, preventing external code from corrupting object state. In frameworks, this allows changing internal optimization or memory layout without breaking user code, enabling rapid iteration and maintaining backward compatibility across versions.

Inheritance enables code reuse and specialization: child classes automatically gain parent behavior and override specific methods. In PyTorch, all 50+ layer types inherit from nn.Module once, reducing code duplication by thousands of lines and ensuring consistent behaviour across the entire framework.

Polymorphism enables writing generic code that works with any object satisfying an interface. A training loop that calls model.forward() works whether the model contains Conv, LSTM, or Transformer layers—the correct implementation runs automatically, making code modular and extensible.

Method Resolution Order (MRO) determines which method executes when called on an object. Python's C3 linearization ensures consistent, predictable resolution even with complex inheritance, allowing frameworks to safely extend classes without surprising behavior.

Composition is often preferable to inheritance: instead of inheriting from multiple parents, a class contains instances of other objects. A model contains layers (composition) rather than inheriting from them; this is more flexible and avoids fragile multiple inheritance hierarchies.

The Template Method pattern enforces consistent workflows while allowing specialization: a base class defines the process skeleton (analyze = gather → evaluate → recommend) while subclasses implement only their unique steps. This is how frameworks ensure all layers follow consistent initialization and execution pipelines.

sections.content: A developer is building a machine learning framework and needs to support many different layer types (Conv2D, LSTM, Dropout, BatchNorm). Rather than implementing each from scratch, they decide to create a base Layer class that handles weight initialization, gradient computation, device placement, and serialization. Each specific layer type then inherits from Layer and implements only its forward() computation. What OOP principle is the developer using to eliminate code duplication?
items: Polymorphism - allowing different layers to respond to the same forward() call differently
Inheritance - having specific layers inherit common functionality from the base Layer class
Encapsulation - hiding the internal weight storage behind a public forward() interface
Abstraction - treating all layers as abstract mathematical operations
sections.explanation: The developer is leveraging inheritance. Each layer type (Conv2D, LSTM, Dropout) inherits from the base Layer class, automatically gaining all the common functionality (weight initialization, gradient computation, device placement, serialization) defined in Layer. Each specific layer then overrides the forward() method with its unique computation. This eliminates duplicating initialization, gradient, and serialization code across all 50+ layer types. While polymorphism is involved (different layers implement forward() differently), the code duplication elimination specifically comes from inheritance's code reuse mechanism. Encapsulation and abstraction are also present but don't directly address the duplication problem—inheritance does.

sections.content: In a neural network training system, you have a base Optimizer class that defines the optimization loop: compute_gradients() → apply_learning_rate() → update_parameters(). Different optimizers (SGD, Adam, RMSprop) inherit from Optimizer and override the apply_learning_rate() method with their specific logic. When training code calls optimizer.optimize_step(), which version of apply_learning_rate() executes?
items: Always the base Optimizer.apply_learning_rate(), regardless of which optimizer type is instantiated
The version belonging to the specific optimizer's actual class at runtime (dynamic dispatch / polymorphism)
Whichever version was written first in the inheritance chain
The version that the optimizer was declared as in the source code
sections.explanation: The correct version executes based on the object's actual runtime type, not its declared type—this is dynamic dispatch and is the essence of polymorphism. If the variable 'optimizer' holds an Adam instance, calling optimizer.optimize_step() triggers the optimize_step() method which calls apply_learning_rate(). Python's method lookup determines that optimizer is actually an Adam object, so Adam.apply_learning_rate() executes, not the base class version. This allows training code to work with any optimizer subclass without knowing its type. The alternative (always calling the base version or using declared type) would require explicit if-elif chains checking optimizer types, defeating the purpose of polymorphism and making the code rigid and unmaintainable.
